title: Tahitian negation: how to say “no, not, never, don’t” with 'aita, e'ita, e'ere, 'eiaha
introduction:
Big picture. Languages usually pick a main way to say “no/not.” The guidance we got lists three common routes: (1) a special negative word (like “not” in “This is not a dog”), (2) a negative bit added to a word (like “im-” in “im-possible”), or (3) a special negative verb (the guidance even cites Proto‑Tahitian forms like 'ita/'ere/'ore).
What English does. English mainly uses a special negative word: “not” (as in “This is not a dog”). It can also use words like “no,” and some words with negative bits (“im-possible”).
A hint for Tahitian. Tahitian uses dedicated negative words placed in front of what you are denying. The key ones in our data are **'aita**, **e'ita**, **e'ere**, and **'eiaha**. They sit up front and control the whole clause. Important note: our sources disagree on how to label them. The Sentence Analysis says “most‑frequent pattern: negative verbs/predicates,” while the Documents call them “specialized negative particles.” Both sources describe the same forms and placements in sentences. In this lesson we simply show you how each one works, and we always mark them with **…** so you can spot them easily.

sections.focus: Which negator to choose (and a note on the disagreement between sources)

example.target_sentence: 'aita vau i 'ite
example.description: Whole‑event negation of a completed/actual situation with **'aita + SUBJECT + i + VERB**. Here, **'aita** (‘no/not (actually)’) + **vau** (‘I’) + **i 'ite** (‘knew/know’ in this completed sense).

example.target_sentence: e'ita māua e haere e hopu i te vai
example.description: Future/irrealis or inability with **e'ita + SUBJECT + e + VERB**. Here **e'ita** (‘won’t/can’t’) + **māua** (‘we (2)’) + **e haere e hopu** (‘go bathe’).

example.target_sentence: e'ere rā vau i te tamari'i fa'ahou, 'ua pa'ari au i teienei
example.source_sentence: But I’m not a child any more, I’ve grown up.
example.description: Category/identity negation with **e'ere (SUBJ) i te NOUN**. Here **e'ere** denies the label ‘child’ for ‘me’ anymore.

example.target_sentence: 'eiaha 'oe e ha'ape'ape'a
example.description: Prohibitive (negative imperative) with **'eiaha (+ SUBJECT) e + VERB**. Here **'eiaha** = ‘don’t’. Subject can be present (**'oe** ‘you’) or omitted.

example.target_sentence: 'aita, 'aita e 'ōro'a
example.description: Negative existence with **'aita e + NOUN** (‘there is no X’). Doubling **'aita, 'aita** is a natural “no, no.”

sections.description:
Our two sources disagree on labeling the “most‑frequent pattern”:
- Sentence Analysis: Tahitian mainly uses dedicated negative predicate words that behave like verbal heads (so, “negative verbs/predicates”).
- Documents: Tahitian mainly uses specialized negative particles.
Both describe the same items—**'aita**, **e'ita**, **e'ere**, **'eiaha**—placed first and controlling the clause. So, in practice, pick the form by meaning: **'aita** (actual/past and existence), **e'ita** (future/irrealis or inability), **e'ere** (not a category / not having a quality / not that identity), **'eiaha** (don’t …). Also notice the small markers after the negator: **i** (completed/actual) and **e** (irrealis/non‑past).

example.target_sentence: 'aita 'oe i fārerei a'e nei iāna
example.description: ‘Never (up to now)’ with **'aita + SUBJECT + i + VERB + a'e nei**. The scope is the whole past‑up‑to‑now event: you did not meet him at any time up to now.

example.target_sentence: 'aita i 'itehia mai
example.description: Patient‑focused negation: **'aita i + passive VERB** (‘not found’). The event “finding” did not occur (actual/completed domain).

example.target_sentence: 'aita ato'a ihoā 'oe i taui roa
example.description: Whole event negation with **'aita … i VERB**, plus a degree word (**roa** ‘much’). ‘You did not change much.’

example.target_sentence: e'ita vau e ta'oto maita'i i te pō
example.source_sentence: I can’t sleep well at night.
example.description: Irrealis/inability with **e'ita + SUBJECT + e + VERB**: the sleeping well event won’t/can’t happen.

example.target_sentence: E'ita paha vau e tae
example.description: Irrealis future with **e'ita**, plus **paha** ‘probably’. Whole event is denied in the future/hypothetical space.

sections.description: Use **'aita** with the completed/actual sense and **e'ita** with future/irrealis or inability. Patterns you’ll see a lot: **'aita + SUBJECT + i + VERB** (actual/completed), and **e'ita + SUBJECT + e + VERB** (irrealis/future). The negator sits first; **i** or **e** follows it and comes before the main verb.

example.target_sentence: 'eiaha 'oe e ha'ape'ape'a
example.description: Basic prohibitive with **'eiaha (+ SUBJECT) e + VERB**. ‘Don’t worry.’

example.target_sentence: 'eiaha e haere i te 'ohipa
example.description: Prohibitive with omitted subject: **'eiaha e + VERB**.

example.target_sentence: Eiaha 'oe e ha'amaumau i tā tātou 'ohipa
example.description: Same prohibitive frame: **'eiaha 'oe e VERB …** = ‘don’t …’. The source line is in French as provided.

example.target_sentence: Eiaha e hōro'a i te mā'a 'ōhinu nāna
example.source_sentence: Ne lui donnez pas de la nourriture grasse
example.description: Prohibitive with **'eiaha e VERB …** = ‘don’t give …’. The source line is in French as provided.

example.target_sentence: Eiaha 'oe e pāfa'i i te vī pī !
example.source_sentence: Ne cueille pas des mangues vertes !
example.description: Prohibitive with **'eiaha 'oe e VERB …** = ‘don’t pick …’. The source line is in French as provided.

sections.description: To tell someone not to do something, use **'eiaha** at the front, then (optionally) the subject, then **e**, then the verb: **'eiaha (SUBJ) e VERB**. This is different from English “don’t,” but the force is the same.

sections.focus: There is no … / not here / not there (existential and locative)

example.target_sentence: 'aita, 'aita e 'ōro'a
example.description: Negative existence with **'aita e + NOUN** (‘there is no X’).

example.target_sentence: 'aita e fare tō teie vaeha'a
example.source_sentence: Il n'y a pas de maison ici
example.description: Negative existence in a place. The source line is in French as provided.

example.target_sentence: 'aita atu ra ïa e mā'a toe fa'ahou i te fare
example.source_sentence: now we have nothing left at home!
example.description: ‘No more X left’ with **'aita e + NOUN + toe/ fa'ahou** plus a location. Here: no food left anymore at home.

example.target_sentence: 'aita tō reira
example.description: Absence at a location with **'aita + (tō) + PLACE**.

example.target_sentence: mm, 'aita i 'ō nei
example.description: Absence near the speaker with **'aita i + PLACE**.

sections.description: Use **'aita** to say “there is no …” and to deny presence at a place. English uses “there is no … / it’s not here,” while Tahitian front‑places **'aita** to carry the negative meaning, then adds the noun and the place.

sections.focus: Not a kind / not that identity / not that quality (with e'ere)

example.target_sentence: e'ere rā vau i te tamari'i fa'ahou, 'ua pa'ari au i teienei
example.source_sentence: But I’m not a child any more, I’ve grown up.
example.description: Category negation with **e'ere (SUBJ) i te NOUN** = ‘(SUBJ) is not (a) NOUN.’

example.target_sentence: e'ere ānei tā 'oe puta ha'api'ira'a
example.description: Identification/ownership denied with **e'ere**: ‘Isn’t it your notebook?’

example.target_sentence: 'aita... e'ere i te mea maita'i i teie mau mahana
example.source_sentence: Well… I’m not feeling well these days.
example.description: Quality negation with **e'ere i te mea + quality** (‘not good’).

example.target_sentence: E'ere terā i'a i te roi.
example.source_sentence: This fish is not a grouper.
example.description: Category/label negation with **e'ere … i te NOUN**.

example.target_sentence: E'ere teie 'ohipa i te mea 'ōhie.
example.description: Quality negation with **e'ere … i te mea + quality**.

sections.description: When you deny that something belongs to a kind (not a child, not a policeman) or deny a quality (not easy, not good), use **e'ere**. English uses “not (a) … / not … (adj).” Tahitian uses **e'ere** at the front and then a phrase with **i te …** to name the category or the quality.

example.target_sentence: e riri mai te 'orometua mai te peu ē, 'aita tā'u puta ha'api'ira'a
example.source_sentence: The teacher will be quite angry if I don’t have my notebook.
example.description: ‘Not have X’ with **'aita + tā/ tō + POSSESSOR + NOUN**. Here: **'aita tā'u puta ha'api'ira'a** = ‘I do not have my notebook.’

example.target_sentence: 'ē 'aita ato'a ho'i i nava'i fa'ahou tā māua moni
example.source_sentence: Besides, we don’t have enough money anymore.
example.description: Negation of quantity with **'aita … i nava'i fa'ahou**, plus possessed **tā māua moni** (‘our money (2)’). ‘No longer enough money.’

example.target_sentence: Aita tā tātou e tipi, e tū'ā noa ïa tātou i tā tātou ha'ari uto
example.source_sentence: Nous n'avons pas de couteaux, nous allons ouvrir notre coco germé avec une pierre
example.description: Possession denied with **'aita tā tātou …** = ‘we have no …’. The source line is in French as provided.

example.target_sentence: e'ere ānei tā 'oe puta ha'api'ira'a
example.description: Ownership as identity questioned: **e'ere …** asks ‘isn’t it (yours)?’

example.target_sentence: Aita ā tā'u mau tārahu i pe'e
example.source_sentence: Je n'ai pas encore acquitté toutes mes dettes.
example.description: Negated possession with ‘not yet’: **'aita ā …** ‘not yet’. The source line is in French as provided.

sections.description: To say you don’t have something, Tahitian uses the existence frame with a possessor: **'aita + tā/tō + POSSESSOR + NOUN** (‘there is no (X) to me/with me’). To deny identity/ownership (‘This is not mine’), use **e'ere … i te …**. For amounts, you’ll see **'aita … i nava'i** (‘not enough’), often with **fa'ahou** (‘anymore/no longer’).

sections.focus: Time/aspect words with negation: no longer, never, not yet

example.target_sentence: 'aita 'oe i fārerei a'e nei iāna
example.description: ‘Never (up to now)’ with **'aita … i VERB + a'e nei**.

example.target_sentence: 'aita atu ra ïa e mā'a toe fa'ahou i te fare
example.source_sentence: now we have nothing left at home!
example.description: ‘No more/none left’ with **'aita e NOUN + toe/ fa'ahou**.

example.target_sentence: e fare ha'api'ira'a tahito, 'aita fa'ahou i teienei
example.source_sentence: That was an old school that doesn’t exist anymore.
example.description: Cessation with **fa'ahou** under negation: **'aita fa'ahou …** ‘no longer (now).’

example.target_sentence: 'ē 'aita ato'a ho'i i nava'i fa'ahou tā māua moni
example.source_sentence: Besides, we don’t have enough money anymore.
example.description: ‘No longer enough’ with **'aita … i nava'i fa'ahou**.

example.target_sentence: 'ua 'ite a'ena 'oe
example.description: Positive counterpart for contrast (from the analysis): **a'ena** = ‘already’. Useful to feel the difference with **a'e nei** (‘ever/so far’) and **fa'ahou** (‘again/anymore’).

sections.description: Tahitian adds little time words to sharpen meaning under negation: **fa'ahou** (‘anymore/no longer’), **a'e nei** (‘ever/so far → never under negation’), and **ā** (‘still/yet’ in negative possession like **'aita ā …**). This is how you say ‘no more …,’ ‘never …,’ or ‘not yet ….’

example.target_sentence: 'aita vau i 'ite
example.description: Sentence (whole predicate) negation with **'aita + SUBJ + i + VERB**. The event ‘knowing’ did not happen (actual/completed sense).

example.target_sentence: e'ita māua e haere e hopu i te vai
example.description: Sentence (whole predicate) negation with **e'ita + SUBJ + e + VERB** (future/irrealis).

example.target_sentence: e'ere i te mea maita'i i teie mau mahana
example.source_sentence: I’m not feeling well these days.
example.description: Constituent/label negation: **e'ere i te mea + quality** denies the quality label ‘good.’

example.target_sentence: E'ere terā i'a i te roi.
example.source_sentence: This fish is not a grouper.
example.description: Constituent/label negation: denies inclusion in the category ‘grouper.’

example.target_sentence: e'ere ānei tā 'oe puta ha'api'ira'a
example.description: Constituent/identity negation: denies identification with a specific owner.

sections.description:
- Sentence scope: **'aita** and **e'ita** negate the whole action or state. They take **i** (actual/completed) or **e** (irrealis) right after them.
- Constituent scope (a label/quality/identity): **e'ere** targets just that category/quality/identity. Our Documents note that constituent negation is not the main focus there, but our examples show **e'ere** doing exactly this job.

conclusion:
What to remember. Tahitian says “no/not” by putting one of four words at the start:
- **'aita** for actual/completed facts and for “there is no … / not here/there.” Pattern: **'aita + SUBJECT + i + VERB**; or **'aita e + NOUN** (existence). 
- **e'ita** for future/irrealis and inability. Pattern: **e'ita + SUBJECT + e + VERB**.
- **e'ere** for “not a (kind)/not that identity/not that quality.” Pattern: **e'ere (SUBJ) i te NOUN** or **e'ere i te mea + quality**.
- **'eiaha** for “don’t …” (imperatives). Pattern: **'eiaha (SUBJ) e + VERB**.
Add **i** (actual/completed) or **e** (irrealis) right after the negator and before the verb. Use time words to refine meaning: **fa'ahou** (‘no longer’), **a'e nei** (→ ‘never’ under **'aita**), **ā** (‘not yet’), and remember **'aita** can be doubled as a plain “no.”
Note on sources: one calls these forms negative predicates/verbs, the other calls them specialized negative particles; both describe the same four front‑placed negators with the same sentence patterns. Follow the patterns, and you’ll be fine.

translation_drills.target: 'aita, 'aita e 'ōro'a
translation_drills.source: No, there’s no celebration.

translation_drills.target: 'aita atu ra ïa e mā'a toe fa'ahou i te fare
translation_drills.source: now we have nothing left at home!

translation_drills.target: 'aita tō reira
translation_drills.source: It’s not there.

translation_drills.target: mm, 'aita i 'ō nei
translation_drills.source: Hm, not here.

translation_drills.target: e'ere rā vau i te tamari'i fa'ahou, 'ua pa'ari au i teienei
translation_drills.source: But I’m not a child any more, I’ve grown up.

translation_drills.target: e'ere ānei tā 'oe puta ha'api'ira'a
translation_drills.source: Isn’t it your notebook?

translation_drills.target: 'aita... e'ere i te mea maita'i i teie mau mahana
translation_drills.source: Well… I’m not feeling well these days.

translation_drills.target: 'aita vau i 'ite
translation_drills.source: I don’t know, sister.

translation_drills.target: 'aita 'oe i fārerei a'e nei iāna
translation_drills.source: You’ve never met him.

translation_drills.target: 'aita, 'aita ! e'ita māua e haere e hopu i te vai
translation_drills.source: No, no! We won’t be bathing.

translation_drills.target: e'ita vau e ta'oto maita'i i te pō
translation_drills.source: I can’t sleep well at night.

translation_drills.target: 'eiaha 'oe e ha'ape'ape'a
translation_drills.source: Don’t worry.

translation_drills.target: 'eiaha e haere i te 'ohipa
translation_drills.source: Don’t go to work

translation_drills.target: e fare ha'api'ira'a tahito, 'aita fa'ahou i teienei
translation_drills.source: That was an old school that doesn’t exist anymore.

translation_drills.target: 'ē 'aita ato'a ho'i i nava'i fa'ahou tā māua moni
translation_drills.source: Besides, we don’t have enough money anymore.

translation_drills.target: e riri mai te 'orometua mai te peu ē, 'aita tā'u puta ha'api'ira'a
translation_drills.source: The teacher will be quite angry if I don’t have my notebook.

translation_drills.target: 'aita i 'itehia mai
translation_drills.source: I can't find it.

translation_drills.target: 'aita ato'a ihoā 'oe i taui roa
translation_drills.source: You haven’t changed much!

translation_drills.target: 'aita, 'aita e hota
translation_drills.source: No, I don’t cough.

translation_drills.target: Eiaha 'oe e ha'amaumau i tā tātou 'ohipa
translation_drills.source: Ne retarde pas notre travail

translation_drills.target: e'ita vau e au i te 'āti'ati'a : mea hau'a to'ato'a
translation_drills.source: Je n'aime pas la chair du poisson chèvre, il a une forte odeur de corail.

translation_drills.target: e mea 'ē tāna ravera'a
translation_drills.source: il n'agit pas comme tout le monde.

translation_drills.target: Aita tā tātou e tipi, e tū'ā noa ïa tātou i tā tātou ha'ari uto
translation_drills.source: Nous n'avons pas de couteaux, nous allons ouvrir notre coco germé avec une pierre

translation_drills.target: Eiaha 'oe e ha'ama'au mai iā'u !
translation_drills.source: N'abuse pas !

translation_drills.target: Eiaha e hōro'a i te mā'a 'ōhinu nāna
translation_drills.source: Ne lui donnez pas de la nourriture grasse

translation_drills.target: Aita e fare tō teie vaeha'a
translation_drills.source: Il n'y a pas de maison ici

translation_drills.target: Eiaha e 'amu fa'ahou i tenā mā'a : 'ua ehaeha roa !
translation_drills.source: Ne mangez plus de ce plat, il a mauvais goût parce que conservé trop longtemps !

translation_drills.target: Aita 'oia i ha'amāta'uta'u i te pāto'i i tō rātou mana'o
translation_drills.source: Il n'a pas hésité à s'opposer à leur manière de voir

translation_drills.target: Eiaha 'oe e ha'apa'apa'a i te taofe !
translation_drills.source: Ne fais pas brûler le café !

translation_drills.target: Nō pau noa a'e nei te farāoa
translation_drills.source: Il n'y a plus de pain (depuis peu)

translation_drills.target: Eiaha e fa'ao'ō'o iāna, 'a ta'i
translation_drills.source: Ne vous moquez pas de lui, il va pleurer

translation_drills.target: 'Ua tatīti'a 'o Tūnui i te 'āma'a vī, 'aita rā i roa'a, topa iho nei i roto i te 'ānāvai
translation_drills.source: Tūnui s'est soulevé pour attraper la branche de manguier mais il n'a pas réussi et il est tombé dans la rivière

translation_drills.target: Eiaha 'oe e fa'a'apo i teie ta'ata i te vī 'a māu'a !
translation_drills.source: Ne confie pas à cet homme le soin d'attraper les mangues, tu vas perdre des fruits !

translation_drills.target: Eiaha 'oe e pāfa'i i te vī pī !
translation_drills.source: Ne cueille pas des mangues vertes !

translation_drills.target: E'ita paha vau e tae
translation_drills.source: Je n'irai probablement pas

translation_drills.target: E parau ha'amāta'uta'u, e'ita e tupu
translation_drills.source: Ce ne sont que des menaces, elles ne seront pas mises à exécution

translation_drills.target: E'ita vau e ha'apaupau i tō'u aho i te tāpapa ia 'oe
translation_drills.source: Je ne vais pas m'essoufler à te courir après

translation_drills.target: E purūmu hāoaoa, e'ita te pere'o'o e tae
translation_drills.source: C'est une route étroite, les voitures ne peuvent pas passer

translation_drills.target: eiaha 'oe e tāmute mai i tā'u parau
translation_drills.source: ne m'interromps pas.

translation_drills.target: E tāpū repo tā'u, 'aita rā e ope
translation_drills.source: J'ai une pioche mais je n'ai pas de pelle

translation_drills.target: E faraoa tahito tenā, 'eiaha 'oe e rave
translation_drills.source: C'est du pain rassis, ne le prends pas

translation_drills.target: Ua reva atu ra 'oia ma te 'ore e poro'i mai
translation_drills.source: Il est parti sans dire au-revoir

translation_drills.target: Eiaha 'outou e mārō haere i te 'ē'ati'a na !
translation_drills.source: Ne vous disputez pas en chemin !

translation_drills.target: Aita ā tā'u mau tārahu i pe'e
translation_drills.source: Je n'ai pas encore acquitté toutes mes dettes.

translation_drills.target: Eiaha 'oe e fa'aea noa i raro a'e i tenā tumu ha'ari, 'a topahia 'oe e te 'ōpa'a
translation_drills.source: Ne reste pas sous ce cocotier, tu vas recevoir une noix de coco

translation_drills.target: 'Aita e 'ōutu i tai.
translation_drills.source: There is no cape on the seaward side.

translation_drills.target: 'Aita e pape i roto i te fa'ato'eto'era'a.
translation_drills.source: There is no water in the fridge.

translation_drills.target: E'ere 'o Tino i te mūto'i.
translation_drills.source: Tino is not a policeman.

translation_drills.target: E'ita te moa e 'amu i te 'ōfa'i.
translation_drills.source: Chickens don’t eat stones.

translation_drills.target: E'ita 'oe e ora iā'u.
translation_drills.source: You won’t survive me.

translation_drills.target: 'Aita 'ōna i tauturu ia Pito.
translation_drills.source: He didn’t help Pito.

translation_drills.target: 'Eiaha roa 'oe e 'eiā.
translation_drills.source: Don’t steal.

recording_cq_Preparing for the New Year_1716403492_Tahitian_Jacques Vernaudon_Mirose Paia_1748577013.json
